data.title: Is Harmonic (HLIT) Pricing Reflect Its DCF Value After Recent Share Price Weakness
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Harmonic's current share price reflects its true worth, you are not alone. The stock has drawn attention from investors trying to work out if it is now cheap or still priced for caution.
  • The share price closed at US$9.37 recently, with returns of a 5.6% decline over 7 days, 9.4% decline over 30 days, 6.5% decline year to date, 9.0% decline over 1 year and 25.8% decline over 3 years, compared with a 14.3% gain over 5 years.
  • Recent news coverage has focused on Harmonic's position in broadband access and video delivery technology, including its role as a supplier to cable and telecom operators and its progress in software based solutions. This context helps frame why sentiment around the stock and its risk profile may have shifted even without a single headline event driving the move.
  • On our checks, Harmonic scores 2 out of 6 on valuation, with the score based on how many metrics suggest the shares might be undervalued. You can see the full breakdown in our valuation score. Next, we will walk through the main valuation approaches investors often use for this stock and later circle back to a broader way of thinking about value that goes beyond any single model.

Harmonic scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Harmonic Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s value using a required rate of return. It aims to estimate what the entire business could be worth in today’s dollars, based purely on cash it might generate for shareholders.

For Harmonic, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow (FCF) is about $99.6 million. Analysts have provided explicit FCF estimates out to 2027, with Simply Wall St extrapolating further to create a ten year path. By 2035, the projection used in the model is FCF of $233.7 million, with each future year discounted back to a present value in dollars.

When all projected and discounted cash flows are added together, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $28.94 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $9.37, this implies an intrinsic discount of 67.6%, which points to the shares looking undervalued on this DCF view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Harmonic is undervalued by 67.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 47 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Harmonic.

Approach 2: Harmonic Price vs Sales

For companies that are profitable but may have earnings that move around, the P/S ratio is often a useful sense check because sales are usually less volatile than earnings and less affected by accounting choices.

Growth expectations and risk still matter though. A higher growth outlook or lower perceived risk can justify a higher “normal” P/S multiple, while slower growth or higher risk tends to support a lower one.

Harmonic currently trades on a P/S of 2.87x. That sits above the Communications industry average of 1.92x and also above the peer average of 2.30x. On those simple comparisons, the shares look more expensive than many sector peers.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Harmonic is 1.76x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/S might be, given factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and specific risks. Because it blends these into one figure, it can be more tailored than a broad industry or peer comparison, which treats all companies as broadly similar.

Comparing Harmonic’s current 2.87x P/S to the 1.76x Fair Ratio suggests the shares are trading above what this framework would imply.

Result: OVERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Harmonic Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. On Simply Wall St you can use Narratives on the Harmonic Community page to attach a clear story to your own numbers, link that story to a forecast for revenue, earnings and margins, translate it into a fair value you can compare with the current share price, and let the tool automatically refresh that fair value when new news or earnings arrive. One investor might anchor on a more cautious Harmonic view with a Fair Value near US$8.00, while another leans toward a more optimistic view closer to US$15.00. Both can quickly see how their chosen Narrative lines up against today’s market price when deciding whether the stock looks attractive, fully priced or expensive to them.

For Harmonic, however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Harmonic Narratives:

🐂 Harmonic Bull Case

  • Small-cap communications equipment company in video delivery and broadband, competing with larger players across broadband access, cable edge and streaming tools.
  • Views the current P/E of 18.1 as low versus an industry P/E of 32.26, with recent Broadband and Video segment metrics, margins and SaaS shifts used to argue the stock may be undervalued relative to peers.
  • Highlights both tailwinds such as broadband expansion, 5G and virtualized networks, and risks such as execution, customer concentration and Video segment pressures, framing HLIT as a potential opportunity with moderate risk.

🐻 Harmonic Bear Case

  • Builds a case around modest revenue growth, lower future profit margins, a P/E multiple of 15.3x by about 2028 and a fair value of US$8.00, which is below the recent share price.

data.title: How H&R Block’s Returning Hope Milestone Shapes Its Investment Story
data.description:
• H&R Block Canada marked the 10 year anniversary of its Returning Hope free tax clinic program.
  • The initiative has supported low income and vulnerable Canadians with free tax filing services over the past decade.
  • The program has returned $6 million in refunds and credits to eligible participants since launch.

For investors following NYSE:HRB, this kind of community program adds a different angle to a company best known for personal tax preparation, digital filing tools and related advisory services. While most headlines focus on earnings and product updates, ongoing service to underserved taxpayers can shape how customers view the brand during a core annual touchpoint such as tax season.

In the future, you can monitor how H&R Block communicates any changes in the growth, scope or evolution of Returning Hope alongside its broader product mix. Any disclosures on participation levels, partnerships or expanded services could help you better understand how community programs fit alongside commercial priorities for the business.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for H&R Block by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on H&R Block.

4 things going right for H&R Block that this headline doesn't cover.

For H&R Block, the 10 year run of Returning Hope in Canada highlights how its assisted tax network can be used for both commercial and social goals. Funding more than 100 paid tax professionals to work in 26 community support centers is not directly about near term revenue, but it can strengthen brand trust with people who often have complex benefits situations and limited access to advice. For a business that competes with Intuit, TurboTax and other low cost digital options, that kind of goodwill at tax time can matter for client retention and referrals, especially as some participants may later move into paid services as their finances change.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The H&R Block Narrative

  • The focus on hands on tax advice for vulnerable Canadians lines up with the broader narrative around growing tax complexity and the need for professional help, reinforcing H&R Block’s role where credits and benefits are critical.
  • At the same time, dedicating paid staff to free clinics may pressure costs if not carefully managed, which sits against the narrative that emphasizes efficiency gains and margin discipline.
  • The Canadian clinic initiative does not explicitly feature digital tools or AI powered support, so the potential to link these free services with H&R Block’s hybrid and online offerings may not be fully captured in the existing narrative.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for H&R Block to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 2 key risks for H&R Block, including negative shareholders’ equity, which can limit financial flexibility if conditions become less favorable.
  • ⚠️ The company also carries a high level of debt, so any community program that adds fixed costs without clear offsets may feed into existing balance sheet concerns.
  • 🎁 H&R Block is described as trading at good value relative to peers and industry, and this kind of long running community program can support the brand that underpins those cash flows.
  • 🎁 The business has been flagged for a high and reliable dividend and a history of earnings growth, which may give some investors more comfort when management funds initiatives that build long term customer relationships.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching whether H&R Block shares more detail on client outcomes from Returning Hope, such as how many participants later use paid services or engage with its AI powered and hybrid products. Any commentary on cost, scale or partnerships around the clinics could show how repeatable the model is in Canada or other markets. You can also track whether management connects programs like this to its broader push into small business services and digital tools, since that would help clarify if these efforts sit at the edge of the business or are part of a wider plan.

data.title: Borr Drilling Rig Disruptions Test Revenue Resilience In Arabian Gulf
data.description:
• Borr Drilling's Arabian Gulf offshore operations have been disrupted following recent regional hostilities.
  • Multiple rigs have been precautionarily down manned, with staff reduced to essential personnel only.
  • The Arabia III rig has been fully shut down and evacuated after an incident on a customer operated platform.
  • These actions pose a material risk to current revenue streams and raise questions about operational safety and geopolitical exposure.

For investors watching NYSE:BORR, this is an operational story rather than just a share price headline. The stock most recently closed at $5.05, after a 12.9% decline over the past week and an 8.0% decline over the past month, while still showing a 26.6% gain year to date and a 137.1% gain over the past year. Those returns sit alongside disruption risk that is concentrated in a key operating region.

What matters now is how long these rigs remain partially staffed or offline, what that means for contract terms, and whether insurance or customer arrangements soften the financial impact. Investors will likely watch for updates on when normal operations can resume, how the company addresses safety concerns, and whether this incident leads to changes in how Borr Drilling structures its exposure to geopolitically sensitive areas.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Borr Drilling by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Borr Drilling.

📰 Beyond the headline: 3 risks and 2 things going right for Borr Drilling that every investor should see.

Operationally, this update underlines that Borr’s revenue opportunity in the Arabian Gulf is closely tied to geopolitics and customer safety policies, not just rig performance. With three jack up rigs in Qatar and the UAE down manned and Arabia III fully shut down and evacuated, contracted work is effectively on pause or running at reduced capacity. That can affect near term revenue recognition and utilization, even if contracts remain in place. For investors comparing Borr with peers like Valaris, Seadrill or Shelf Drilling, this is a reminder that concentration in any one region or client set can quickly change the risk profile, especially when a large part of the fleet is located in shallow water fields in sensitive areas.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Borr Drilling Narrative

  • The incident directly ties into the existing narrative that political and environmental risks can affect shallow water project activity, which is already a key theme for how investors think about Borr’s long term contract pipeline.
  • It also challenges the view that strong contract momentum in regions like the Middle East automatically leads to steady utilization, since precautionary shutdowns can interrupt otherwise firm commitments.
  • The specific operational disruption on a customer operated platform and the down manning of rigs in multiple countries is not fully reflected in earlier discussion about contract coverage, which focused more on pricing, demand and leverage than on near term operational interruptions.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Borr Drilling to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Concentration of several rigs in the Arabian Gulf means regional hostilities and customer precautionary measures can quickly affect utilization and current revenue streams.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have highlighted that interest payments are not well covered by earnings, so any disruption to cash generation can make the capital structure more demanding to support.
  • 🎁 Borr operates a modern jack up fleet in a segment where underinvestment in new supply and retirement of older units can support long term demand for capable rigs.
  • 🎁 Existing contract coverage across multiple regions, including the Middle East, Mexico and Southeast Asia, provides diversified exposure that may help offset disruptions in any single basin over time.

data.description:

Eli Lilly, known for treatments in diabetes, obesity care and other chronic conditions, is pushing deeper into obesity medicine with oral GLP-1 candidates such as orforglipron. The new China-focused investment comes amid strong global interest in weight management drugs and broader efforts by pharmaceutical companies to secure reliable production for high-demand therapies.

For you as an investor, this move highlights how Eli Lilly is tying manufacturing, partnerships and regulatory filings together in a single market entry plan. The scale and length of the commitment in China may influence how you think about the company’s international footprint, exposure to Asian healthcare markets and potential execution risks around a large, multi-year build out.

This US$3b, decade-long commitment in China signals that Eli Lilly is treating orforglipron as a core global product rather than a side bet. By localizing oral solid-dose manufacturing and locking in capacity through Pharmaron, Lilly is trying to reduce supply risk and shorten distribution routes for China and potentially other Asian markets. For you, the key point is that this is not just about one drug; it is a build-out of a China-focused value chain that could later support other small-molecule medicines. At the same time, it adds execution questions, including regulatory timelines, local partner performance, and policy shifts that could affect foreign pharma in China. The move sits alongside Lilly’s U.S. access work for GLP-1 drugs, such as Employer Connect, so you are seeing both demand-side and supply-side investments around obesity care. How well Lilly balances capital going into China, Korea Gateway Labs and Japanese capacity with legal and pricing pressures at home will influence how durable this obesity-focused business model looks over time.

How This Fits Into The Eli Lilly Narrative

  • The China build-out supports the narrative theme that obesity and diabetes therapies anchor Lilly’s future. Global manufacturing expansion and supply chain hubs are described as key enablers of cardiometabolic growth.
  • Heavy capital spending tied to a small group of GLP-1 products also echoes a concern in the narrative around concentration risk, as more of Lilly’s cash and attention go toward a single therapy class.
  • The plan to use China as an Asian base for orforglipron and other pipeline drugs adds geographic and operational detail that the narrative only touches on at a high level when it talks about global expansion.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it makes sense to watch three things closely: the progress of orforglipron’s regulatory review in China, any updates on construction milestones and utilization plans for the Suzhou and Pharmaron-linked facilities, and how management talks about capital allocation across China, Korea Gateway Labs, Japanese expansion and U.S. plants. data.title: Charter Expands RingCentral Alliance To Deepen AI Role In Business Services
data.description:
• Charter Communications (NasdaqGS:CHTR) and RingCentral have expanded their partnership to roll out an AI driven unified communications and contact center suite for Spectrum Business customers.
  • The new offering integrates RingCentral’s RingCX and AI Conversation Expert tools, bringing omnichannel capabilities and advanced analytics across voice, video and digital channels.
  • The launch targets mid market and enterprise clients that use Spectrum Business services and want more data driven insight from their customer interactions.

For you as an investor, this move sits at the intersection of telecom infrastructure and cloud based communications software. Charter is using its Spectrum Business footprint to offer more than connectivity by adding AI powered tools that can plug into existing workflows and CRM systems. This reflects a broader theme across business communications in which buyers look for a single provider to handle managed networks and front line customer engagement tools.

The expanded RingCentral partnership may also affect how Charter positions Spectrum Business relative to other cable and telecom peers that sell into mid market and enterprise accounts. A key factor will be how effectively Charter can translate this AI centric suite into deeper customer relationships and wider adoption across its existing business client base.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Charter Communications by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Charter Communications.

We've flagged 1 risk for Charter Communications. See which could impact your investment.

For Charter, this expanded RingCentral deal leans into a higher value role with business customers, not just a pipe for connectivity. UCX with RingCentral lets Spectrum Business wrap managed network services, unified communications, and AI-driven contact center tools into one contract, which can make Charter more relevant in IT decision making against players like Comcast Business and AT&T. Because the platform runs over Charter’s managed network, the company is tying its core infrastructure to software that touches sales execution and customer service, which can increase switching costs for mid-market and enterprise clients if it gains traction.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Charter Communications Narrative

  • The move supports the existing catalyst that Charter is using AI and network investments to improve service efficiency, since RingCX and ACE are designed to cut manual work and surface issues across customer interactions.
  • It could challenge the view that cost control is the main operational lever, because delivering and supporting AI-heavy contact center solutions may require ongoing spending on support, integration, and sales resources.
  • The narrative focuses heavily on residential broadband, mobile, and network upgrades, while this UCX offering adds another angle in business and government services that may not be fully reflected in current expectations.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Charter Communications to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ If Spectrum Business and RingCentral cannot win share against established enterprise providers such as Cisco and Microsoft, the return on Charter’s sales and support effort for UCX could be limited.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged interest coverage as a key risk, so additional investment in business services has to be weighed against existing leverage and the financing needs around the Cox Communications deal.
  • 🎁 A broader portfolio that combines managed networks with AI-powered contact center tools may help Charter deepen relationships with existing clients and reduce churn, especially where competitors only sell connectivity.
  • 🎁 If UCX adoption grows, Charter could gain better visibility into business customer behavior through RingCentral’s analytics, which may support more targeted product bundling and upsell opportunities over time.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching how quickly Charter can sign UCX deployments with Spectrum Business customers and whether those contracts expand over time to include more seats and features. Pay attention to management commentary on business churn, ARPU, and cross-sell of managed network services alongside UCX, as that will show whether the bundle is resonating versus offerings from Comcast, AT&T, and others. Given Charter’s leverage and Cox acquisition plans, investors may also want to track any disclosure on incremental capital or operating costs tied to UCX and how those sit within overall cash flow priorities.
